intro:
The Transfiguration of Jesus is narrated in all three Synoptic Gospels (Matt. 17:1-9; Mark 9:2-10; Luke 9:28-36) and alluded to in 2 Peter 1:16-18. Jesus took Peter, James, and John up a high mountain (traditionally identified with Mount Tabor, though Mount Hermon has also been proposed) and was 'transfigured' before them — the Greek metamorphoo indicating a change of outward form: his face shone like the sun, his garments became dazzling white, and Moses and Elijah appeared conversing with him about 'his departure (exodos) which he was about to accomplish in Jerusalem' (Luke 9:31). A bright cloud overshadowed the disciples, and the divine voice declared: 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased. Listen to him' (Matt. 17:5) — combining Psalm 2:7 (royal installation) and Isaiah 42:1 (the Servant) with a command superseding Moses's authority.

The Transfiguration is theologically multi-dimensional. The presence of Moses and Elijah represents the Law and the Prophets bearing witness to Jesus, while the divine command 'Listen to him' declares him the final prophet greater than Moses (Deut. 18:15). The luminous transformation briefly discloses the divine glory that the Son 'emptied himself' of in the incarnation (Phil. 2:7) — the glory he shared with the Father 'before the world was created' (John 17:5). The Shekinah-cloud echoes the glory cloud of the wilderness tabernacle (Ex. 40:34-38) and Sinai theophany (Ex. 24:15-18). Peter's proposal to build three shelters (tabernacles) 'not knowing what he said' (Luke 9:33) reflects Feast of Tabernacles imagery of divine dwelling. Placed immediately after the first passion prediction, the Transfiguration insists that divine glory and self-giving suffering belong inseparably together in Jesus's person and mission — the context in which 2 Peter 1:16-18 cites it as eyewitness evidence for 'his majesty.'
